record_office_terminology.land_records: County Recorder (deeds, mortgages, liens, plats -- one office per county)
record_office_terminology.probate: Clerk of District Court (wills, estates, guardianships, adoptions -- Iowa has a unified court system)
record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: County Recorder (marriage licenses and certificates; county-level records)
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1880 (state registration began; compliance improved after 1900; county records often earlier). Iowa Department of Public Health holds state copies from 1880.

burned_county_authority: Iowa has no significant burned counties. Record survival is excellent across all 99 counties. State Historical Society of Iowa (Des Moines and Iowa City) and Iowa State Archives in Des Moines serve as central repositories for backup and transferred records.
administrative_notes: Iowa has 99 counties, all organized between 1834 and 1857. Iowa was part of Michigan Territory (1834-1836), Wisconsin Territory (1836-1838), and Iowa Territory (1838-1846) before achieving statehood on December 28, 1846. Iowa entered the Union as a free state under the Missouri Compromise. It is a public land state -- original land was transferred from federal ownership through the General Land Office (patents searchable at glorecords.blm.gov). The state has strong Scandinavian (Norwegian, Swedish, Danish), German, Dutch, Czech/Bohemian, and Irish immigrant heritage reflected in church records and ethnic organizations. Coal mining communities in south-central Iowa attracted English, Welsh, Swedish, and Italian miners. The Meskwaki (Sac and Fox) Settlement near Tama is Iowa's only federally recognized tribal community -- unique because the Meskwaki purchased their own land in 1857 rather than being assigned a reservation. The State Historical Society of Iowa has dual locations in Des Moines and Iowa City. The Iowa Genealogical Society (IGS) in Des Moines is the state's largest genealogical organization. County Recorder offices hold land records; Clerk of District Court handles probate and court records.

core_repositories.repository: State Historical Society of Iowa, Des Moines and Iowa City
core_repositories.description: Iowa's central historical repository with dual locations. Des Moines campus (State of Iowa Historical Building) holds Iowa State Archives, manuscripts, photographs, maps, and government records. Iowa City campus holds the research library, special collections, and Iowa City-area materials. Combined holdings include county records on microfilm, territorial and state government records, military records, and the largest collection of Iowa newspapers.

Iowa's largest genealogical society, located in Des Moines. Maintains surname files, Bible record collections, cemetery transcriptions, county research guides, and a research library. Publishes the Hawkeye Heritage journal. Offers research assistance and has a growing digital collection.

core_repositories.repository: Iowa State Archives, Des Moines (part of SHSI)
core_repositories.description: Official repository for Iowa state government records including governor's records, legislative records, state agency files, institutional records (state hospitals, prisons, schools), and transferred county records. Holds vital records indexes, military service records, and census records.

familysearch_collections.description: Extensive indexed and browsable collections including Iowa county records (deeds, probate, marriages, court records), vital records, church records, cemetery records, and military records. FamilySearch microfilmed extensively at Iowa county courthouses and at the State Historical Society. Key collections include Iowa marriages, probate records, and land records.

Comprehensive research guide with county-specific pages, record availability, and links to digitized microfilm. Covers Iowa's county record system, immigration patterns, and research strategies for Iowa's diverse ethnic communities.

newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Digitized historic Iowa newspapers. Iowa had hundreds of newspapers -- county seats and small towns each typically had at least one weekly paper. German-language and Scandinavian-language newspapers were common. Obituaries, legal notices, and community news are essential for Iowa genealogical research.

SHSI holds the most extensive collection of Iowa newspapers on microfilm. Digital access expanding through partnership with Chronicling America. Includes county weeklies, city dailies, and ethnic-language newspapers.

military_records.source_name: Iowa Military Records -- State Archives / NARA
military_records.repository: Iowa State Archives, Des Moines / NARA
military_records.description: Iowa Adjutant General records, Civil War rosters (Iowa contributed ~76,000 Union troops), Mexican-American War service, Spanish-American War, WWI and WWII records. Iowa State Archives holds mustering rolls, pension records, Soldiers' Bonus files, and Grand Army of the Republic (GAR) post records. Iowa Soldiers' and Sailors' Monument Commission records.

military_records.source_name: Civil War Soldiers and Sailors System -- Iowa
military_records.description: Searchable database of Civil War service records for Iowa Union soldiers. Iowa contributed approximately 76,000 men to the Union army, organized into 48 infantry regiments, 9 cavalry regiments, and 4 artillery batteries.

land_and_property.repository: Iowa County Recorders / Iowa Land Records
land_and_property.description: Statewide online portal for Iowa county land records. Participating counties provide online access to deed indexes and some document images. Coverage varies by county. Each of Iowa's 99 counties has a County Recorder office maintaining land records.

land_and_property.source_name: BLM GLO -- Iowa Federal Land Patents
land_and_property.repository: Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office Records
land_and_property.description: Iowa is a public land state. All original transfers of land from federal to private ownership are documented in GLO patents. Iowa land was surveyed under the rectangular survey system (township, range, section). Cash sales, homesteads, military warrants, railroad grants, and swamp land grants. Searchable online by name and location.

vital_records.source_name: Iowa Department of Public Health -- Vital Records
vital_records.repository: Iowa Department of Public Health, Des Moines
vital_records.description: State vital records: births and deaths from 1880 (registration compliance improved after 1900). Marriage records at county level from county formation. Divorce records through Clerk of District Court. For pre-1880 events, researchers must use church records, cemetery records, newspaper notices, and county records.

church_and_cemetery.repository: Various -- State Historical Society of Iowa, denominational archives, FamilySearch
church_and_cemetery.description: Iowa's diverse immigrant populations produced rich church records. Norwegian Lutheran (Luther College Archives, Decorah), German Lutheran (Wartburg Seminary, Dubuque), German and Irish Catholic (Archdiocese of Dubuque, Diocese of Davenport, Diocese of Des Moines, Diocese of Sioux City), Dutch Reformed (Northwestern College, Orange City), Czech Catholic and fraternal (ZCBJ) records, Quaker meeting records, and Methodist circuit riders. FamilySearch has microfilmed many Iowa church records.

Crowdsourced cemetery transcriptions for Iowa. Coverage is extensive due to active Iowa volunteer community. Iowa Genealogical Society and county societies have published cemetery transcription volumes. Township and church cemeteries are common in rural Iowa.

research_guides_and_societies.description: Statewide genealogical society with research library in Des Moines. Publishes Hawkeye Heritage journal. County chapters throughout the state. Surname files, Bible records, cemetery transcriptions, and county research guides.

Volunteer-contributed county-level genealogy resources for all 99 Iowa counties. Includes cemetery transcriptions, Bible records, surname queries, county histories, and research guides. Quality and depth varies by county.

historical_counties_extinct.description: Iowa has several extinct or renamed counties from the territorial period. Bancroft County (1851) was renamed Kossuth County in 1851. Fox County (1851) was renamed Calhoun County in 1853. Risley County (originally Yell County, 1851) was renamed Webster County in 1853. Buncombe County (1851) was renamed Lyon County in 1862. Wahkaw County (proposed 1851) was never organized. Cook County (proposed 1836) became part of other counties. Researchers tracing families in the 1840s-1850s should be aware of these name changes and boundary adjustments. The original two counties (Des Moines and Dubuque, 1834) covered all of present-day Iowa.

immigration_and_naturalization.source_name: Iowa Naturalization Records -- District Courts / NARA
immigration_and_naturalization.repository: Iowa State Archives / NARA Kansas City / County Clerks of District Court
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Naturalization records filed in Iowa district courts from the territorial period. Declarations of intention and petitions for naturalization. Iowa attracted massive immigration -- German, Scandinavian (Norwegian, Swedish, Danish), Dutch, Czech/Bohemian, Irish, English, Welsh, and Luxembourger immigrants. Federal court naturalizations at NARA Kansas City. Many county naturalizations have been microfilmed by FamilySearch.

The most comprehensive Norwegian-American museum in the US. Genealogy department holds family files, church records, immigrant letters (Amerika-breve), ship passenger lists, and bygdebok (community history) references. Essential for researching Norwegian families in Iowa and the Upper Midwest.

native_american_and_tribal_records.repository: NARA Kansas City / Bureau of Indian Affairs / Meskwaki Tribal Government
native_american_and_tribal_records.description: The Meskwaki (Sac and Fox of the Mississippi in Iowa) purchased land near Tama in 1857, creating Iowa's only federally recognized tribal settlement. Unlike reservations, this was a tribal land purchase. BIA agency records at NARA Kansas City include census rolls, allotment records, school records (Sac and Fox Agency School), and correspondence. Tribal government may have additional records. Access to some records may be restricted.

native_american_and_tribal_records.source_name: Iowa Indian Claims and Treaty Records
native_american_and_tribal_records.description: Records related to the Sauk (Sac), Meskwaki (Fox), Ioway, Otoe, Winnebago (Ho-Chunk), and Sioux (Dakota) peoples who inhabited Iowa before European-American settlement. Black Hawk Purchase (1832) and subsequent treaties opened Iowa to settlement. Treaty records, Indian agency records, and removal records at NARA.

burned_counties_summary.notes: Iowa has no burned counties. Record survival across all 99 counties is excellent. Iowa's counties were organized during a relatively short period (1834-1857) and the state experienced no significant courthouse disasters. The State Historical Society of Iowa and Iowa State Archives hold microfilm copies of many county records as backup. FamilySearch also microfilmed extensively at Iowa county offices.
